expectations.text: Uses .persist() to make the aura permanent on canvas
expectations.evidence: `.persist()` appears in both the console prototype (Step 1) and the DAE macro (Step 2).

expectations.text: Uses .name() on every persistent effect for later cleanup
expectations.evidence: Console prototype uses `.name("bless_aura")`, DAE macro uses `.name(`bless_aura_${token.id}`)` for per-token unique naming.

expectations.text: Uses .attachTo() so the aura follows the token
expectations.evidence: `.attachTo(token, { followRotation: false })` in both the prototype and the DAE macro.

expectations.text: Iterates over multiple targets (loop or forEach pattern, not hardcoded single target)
expectations.evidence: Multi-target handling is correctly delegated to DAE: 'Midi-QOL resolves the spell and applies the Bless Active Effect to each target. DAE detects the Active Effect and calls Bless_Aura with "on" for each target.' The macro handles one token per invocation because DAE calls it once per affected token — this is the correct DAE pattern. Step 3 explicitly describes the per-target flow.
Console cleanup: `await Sequencer.EffectManager.endEffects({ name: "bless_aura" });`. DAE off-block: `await Sequencer.EffectManager.endEffects({ origin: args[args.length - 1] });`

expectations.text: Describes the DAE on/off macro pattern (args[0] === 'on' / 'off')
expectations.evidence: DAE macro has `if (args[0] === "on")` and `if (args[0] === "off")` blocks. Explained: 'When the Active Effect is applied to a target, DAE calls Bless_Aura with args[0] = "on". When removed... DAE calls it again with args[0] = "off".'

expectations.text: Explains the TimesUp -> DAE -> Sequencer automatic removal chain
expectations.evidence: Explicitly stated: 'TimesUp expires the duration → DAE removes the Active Effect → DAE fires the macro with "off" → Sequencer ends the persistent effect.' Also has a summary table.

expectations.text: Uses .loopProperty() or similar for the pulsing animation
expectations.evidence: Two `.loopProperty("spriteContainer", "scale.x/y", { from: 0.95, to: 1.05, duration: 1500, pingPong: true, ease: "easeInOutSine" })` calls create a gentle pulse.

claims.claim: Starts with a console prototype before wiring into the module stack
claims.evidence: Step 1 is 'Prototype the visual in the console' with a standalone paste-and-run script before any DAE setup.

claims.claim: Uses origin-based cleanup in the off block rather than name-based
claims.evidence: Off block uses `{ origin: args[args.length - 1] }` and explanation: 'The "off" block uses origin instead of name to clean up — this is more reliable because it's the same identifier DAE passes both times.'
Uses .attachTo() correctly but doesn't explicitly contrast it with .atLocation() or explain why .attachTo() is needed instead.

suggestions.assertion: Iterates over multiple targets (loop or forEach pattern, not hardcoded single target)
suggestions.reason: This assertion is ambiguous for DAE macros. Both with and without skill correctly delegate multi-target iteration to DAE. A more discriminating version: 'Uses per-token unique naming (token.id in the name) to avoid cleanup collisions across multiple targets.'

eval_feedback.overall: Strong pass across all assertions. The with_skill response adds iterative build workflow, loopProperty animation, and console prototyping that the without_skill version lacks.
